summary: Gruber dissects Apple's carefully worded statement to Business Insider about why Xbox Game Pass isn't allowed on iOS, arguing that the media misread it. He contends that Apple's statement isn't saying they can't review each game individually — it's a non-answer that obscures the real policy: game streaming services are simply not allowed in the App Store, period. He points to App Store guideline 4.2.7 as the written rule that prohibits cloud gaming. Gruber characterizes Apple's response as a masterclass in corporate bullshit — every word technically true, but none of it answering the actual question. He speculates the real story may be about platform control rather than money, and finds delicious irony in Microsoft being at the mercy of a platform it doesn't control.
key_points: Business Insider's interpretation that Apple blocked Game Pass because it 'can't review each game' is a reasonable but incorrect reading — Apple never actually stated a reason
App Store guideline 4.2.7 explicitly prohibits game streaming services, making this a written policy rather than a case-by-case judgment
Apple's statement is a textbook non-answer: it describes what IS allowed (native games) rather than explaining why streaming games are banned
The comparison to Netflix and Spotify is a red herring — Apple allows streaming video and music but prohibits streaming software, and simply won't say so plainly
Gruber speculates Apple's motivation is platform control rather than revenue, and finds poetic justice in Microsoft being powerless over the dominant mobile platform
money_quotes: Running this statement through my Applespeak-to-English decoder ring, what I hear is not that they won't allow Xbox Game Pass because they can't review each game separately. What I hear is that game streaming services are not allowed in the iOS App Store. Period, full stop.

It's a perfect example of the difference between bullshit and a lie. Every word in Apple's statement is true, but not a word of it answers why they won't allow Xbox Game Pass or any other cloud game streaming service.

Submitting games individually and appearing in charts and search — presumably App Store charts and search — is just an obfuscated way of saying that native iOS game apps are allowed in the iOS App Store. Well, duh.
Apple would have been much better off saying nothing at all than offering this bullshit non-answer answer, that in fact was so easily and reasonably misconstrued.
The point is that streaming video and music services are allowed in the App Store; streaming software (games or otherwise) is not, unless it works over the web. Apple just doesn't want to say that.
That's not cold and dry — that's a goddamn sizzling hot juicy steak of a story. That's personal.

Not having any control over the world's most lucrative computing platform and wanting something from the company that does — and which has a real taste for exerting its dictatorial control over said platform in mercurial fashion — would fucking suck, wouldn't it?
key_insight: Apple's statement about Xbox Game Pass is not a policy explanation but a deliberate non-answer — the real policy is that game streaming services are flatly prohibited on iOS, and Apple simply refuses to say so plainly.
